         }

         xCoeff += coeff;

      }

   }

   return new int[] {xCoeff, constTerm};

}

      }

   }

   return new int[] {xCoeff, constTerm};

}

Now, code for the Solution class:

Implement the helper function inside the class. 

But in Java, the helper function can be a private method. 

So the code would be:

class Solution {

    public String solveEquation(String equation) {

        String[] parts = equation.split("=");

        String leftPart = parts[0];

        String rightPart = parts[1];

        int[] left = parse(leftPart);

        int[] right = parse(rightPart);

        int coefficientX = left[0] - right[0];

        int constantTerm = right[1] - left[1];

        if (coefficientX == 0) {

            if (constantTerm == 0) {

                return "Infinite solutions";

            } else {

                return "No solution";

            }

        } else {

            int xValue = constantTerm / coefficientX;

            return "x=" + xValue;

        }

    }

    private int[] parse(String part) {

        int xCoeff = 0, constTerm = 0;

        String[] tokens = part.split("(?=[+-])");

        for (String token : tokens) {

            if (token.endsWith("x")) {

                String coeffStr = token.substring(0, token.length() - 1);

                int coeff;

                if (coeffStr.isEmpty()) {

                    coeff = 1;

                } else if (coeffStr.equals("+")) {

                    coeff = 1;

                } else if (coeffStr.equals("-")) {

                    coeff = -1;

                } else {

                    coeff = Integer.parseInt(coeffStr);

                }

                xCoeff += coeff;

            } else {

                constTerm += Integer.parseInt(token);

            }

        }

        return new int[] {xCoeff, constTerm};

    }

}

Example 1: equation is "x+5-3+x=6+x-2". 

Left part is parsed as xCoeff 2, const 2. Right part is parsed as xCoeff 1 (from "+x") and const 6-2=4. 

coefficientX is 2-1=1. constantTerm is 4-2=2. x=2. 

Output is "x=2". 

Example 2: equation is "x=x". coefficientX is 0, constantTerm 0 → infinite. 

Example 3: equation is "2x=x". coefficientX is 2-1=1. constantTerm is 0. x=0. 

All correct. 

Now, code should work. 

Now, check for possible errors. 

What if a token is "0x"? It's parsed as 0. 

Yes. 

What if a token is "+0x"? coeffStr is "+0" → parsed as 0. 

Yes. 

Another case: equation is "2x+3=3+x". 

Left xCoeff 2, const 3. Right xCoeff 1, const 3. 

coefficientX is 2-1=1. constantTerm is 3-3=0 → x=0. 

Output is x=0. 

Yes. 

Now, code should handle all cases. 

Now, time and space complexity. 

Time complexity: O(n), where n is the length of the equation. Splitting and processing each part takes O(n) time. 

Space complexity: O(n) for splitting into tokens. 

Efficiency is HIGH. 

Energy implications: LOW, since it's O(n) time and space. 

So the code should be correct.

solution: ```java
class Solution {
    public String solveEquation(String equation) {
        String[] parts = equation.split("=");
        String leftPart = parts[0];
        String rightPart = parts[1];
        
        int[] left = parse(leftPart);
        int[] right = parse(rightPart);
        
        int coefficientX = left[0] - right[0];
        int constantTerm = right[1] - left[1];
        
        if (coefficientX == 0) {
            return constantTerm == 0 ? "Infinite solutions" : "No solution";
        } else {
            return "x=" + (constantTerm / coefficientX);
        }
    }
    
    private int[] parse(String part) {
        int xCoeff = 0, constTerm = 0;
        String[] tokens = part.split("(?=[+-])");
        
        for (String token : tokens) {
            if (token.endsWith("x")) {
                String coeffStr = token.substring(0, token.length() - 1);
                int coeff;
                if (coeffStr.isEmpty()) {
                    coeff = 1;
                } else if (coeffStr.equals("+")) {
                    coeff = 1;
                } else if (coeffStr.equals("-")) {
                    coeff = -1;
                } else {
                    coeff = Integer.parseInt(coeffStr);
                }
                xCoeff += coeff;
            } else {
                constTerm += Integer.parseInt(token);
            }
        }
        return new int[] {xCoeff, constTerm};
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution parses each side of the equation into coefficients for x and constants. It then rearranges the equation to solve for x. The parsing handles x terms and constants efficiently using string splitting and regex. The algorithm runs in linear time relative to the input size, making it optimal.
